summarization.a: Figure shows synchrotron radiation XRD patterns of PE-ALD Co films on Si(001) after rapid thermal annealing (400–900 °C). The as-deposited film exhibits no distinct peaks, indicating an amorphous or poorly crystalline Co layer. With increasing annealing temperature, diffraction peaks corresponding to β-Co (111) appear first, followed by the emergence and strengthening of CoSi₂ (111), (220), (210), and (211) reflections at ≥700–800 °C, confirming the formation of highly crystalline CoSi₂ at elevated temperatures.

a.question: Does rapid thermal annealing induce phase transformation of PE-ALD Co films on Si into cobalt silicide?

b.question: At what annealing temperature range do CoSi₂ diffraction peaks become dominant?

d.question: Why is the formation of crystalline CoSi₂ important for semiconductor device applications?
d.answer: Crystalline CoSi₂ is a low-resistivity, thermally stable silicide commonly used as a contact material in CMOS technology. Its formation demonstrates that PE-ALD Co films can be converted into technologically relevant silicides through post-deposition annealing, validating the process for integration into advanced semiconductor devices.

c.question: Which structural phases are observed as annealing temperature increases?
c.answer: Amorphous or poorly crystalline Co (as-deposited), β-Co (111) at intermediate temperatures, CoSi₂ (111, 220, 210, 211) at high temperatures
